narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 2.70, an LSAT score of 27, middle-family income background, part-time enrollment plans, and school deciles of 4th in Year 1 and 3rd in Year 3 based on your grades. The admissions model, trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicted a 56.9% chance that you would fail it on your first try—crossing the threshold that led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass and succeed in their legal careers. This prediction wasn't random; it hinged on a few key aspects of your profile that stood out the most to the model. The strongest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your plan to attend part-time, which is uncommon—only about 7% of students do so—and the model sees it as a major hurdle, likely because part-time students in the data often faced greater challenges balancing commitments and passing the bar. Next in influence was your 3rd-decile ranking in Year 3 grades, which is below the typical 5.5-decile average and signaled to the model a dip in academic momentum that correlates with bar struggles. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.70, while lower than the 3.21 average, actually worked slightly in your favor by pulling the prediction a bit toward passing, suggesting the model views it as less of a barrier compared to your other factors. Overall, your profile compares to typical students by having solid but not standout academics and the rarity of part-time status, which amplified the concerns despite your middle-income background and other details. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Hispanic man pursuing law—many from similar backgrounds thrive—but the model flagged these elements as higher-risk based on patterns in past bar outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the 36.7 average, strengthening your Year 3 performance if possible, or exploring full-time options to align better with successful profiles. You might also gather letters emphasizing your resilience and part-time feasibility, or apply to programs with flexible tracks that value diverse experiences like yours. With targeted improvements, you can reshape how models—and admissions teams—see your strong candidacy. Keep pushing; your determination will make the difference.
